coredata.dc:title: Preparation of lysosomal targeted fluorescent carbon dots and its applications in multi-color cell imaging and information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xiaoxiao
Zhang, Yan
Fu, Zheng
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by secondary hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and folic acid as precursors. It had stable properties, small particle size and good water solubility, and its absolute quantum yield was up to 19.48%. Moreover, the emission centers of CDs shifted from green to yellow under different excitation and were successfully used for multi-color cell imaging and targeting of lysosomes. The CDs also showed different color in 254 nm and 365 nm UV light, which could be used to a fluorescent ink for information encryption. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched at pH 6–8 and showed a good linear relationship with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9989, which had been implemented for the measurement of pH in tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-color
Lysosomes
Information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China - Union Foundation of Henan and the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province ( U1704170 , 192102310300 ), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education ( 20A610005 , 21A180015 ) and the doctor initiated project funding of Henan Normal University ( QD18083 ).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan and the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province (U1704170, 192102310300), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education (20A610005, 21A180015) and the doctor initiated project funding of Henan Normal University (QD18083).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy images of CDs (inset: Particle size distribution) (b) FTIR spectrum of CDs (c) Full survey XPS spectra of CDs and high-resolution spectrum of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs with different excitation (400–530 nm) (b) PL and UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs (inset: the picture of CDs solution under 365 nm UV light) (c) PL spectra of diluted CDs (inset: the picture of diluted CDs solution under 365 nm UV light) The effect of (d) different metal ions (e) different amino acids (f) sodium chloride concentration on CDs.
(a) The effect of fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH conditions (b) Linear relationship of F/F0 with pH 6-8.
(a) Cellular toxicity of CDs (b) The fluorescence images of HepG2 cells with 458 nm and 515 nm laser excitation.
The confocal image of HepG2 with (a) commercial lysosomal dye staining and (b) CDs (c) The merged image (d) Emission intensity profiles.
Images with (a) 365 nm UV light and (b) 254 nm UV light.
Measurement of pH in tap water.

head.ce:title: Preparation of lysosomal targeted fluorescent carbon dots and its applications in multi-color cell imaging and information encryption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Life Sciences, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by secondary hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and folic acid as precursors. It had stable properties, small particle size and good water solubility, and its absolute quantum yield was up to 19.48%. Moreover, the emission centers of CDs shifted from green to yellow under different excitation and were successfully used for multi-color cell imaging and targeting of lysosomes. The CDs also showed different color in 254 nm and 365 nm UV light, which could be used to a fluorescent ink for information encryption. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched at pH 6–8 and showed a good linear relationship with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9989, which had been implemented for the measurement of pH in tap water.

Scheme 1. Schematic diagram of preparation of multi-color carbon dots by secondary hydrothermal method and its applications.
Schematic diagram of preparation of multi-color carbon dots by secondary hydrothermal method and its applications
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are small fluorescence carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm [1,2]. Discovered by XU et al. [3] in 2004 when purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes, carbon dots have excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility, etc [4,5]. In recent years, carbon dots are developed for more applications, such as sensing detection, information encryption, cell imaging, drug delivery, photocatalytic degradation, promote plant growth and antimicrobial materials [6–20]. Although carbon dots have many excellent properties, most of them can only emit blue fluorescence of a single color, which limit their further applications. Therefore, finding a method to synthesize polychromatic long-wavelength emitting carbon dots has far-reaching significance for its development. In recent years, researchers have successfully synthesized multi-color emission carbon dots by adopting various methods. For example, Zhao et al. [21] synthesized a yellow emission carbon dots (Y-CDs) with o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, and then successfully synthesized red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) by adding phosphoric acid in the same way. The fabricated R-CDs had excellent ability to inhibit tumor growth. Yan et al. [22] treated phenol derivatives by solvothermal method and synthesized blue-to-red emitting CDs by changing the reaction time, which could be applied to sensors and optoelectronic devices. Dang et al. [23] synthesized N and Cl co-doped CQD through a pot of solvothermal, and obtained panchromatic carbon quantum dots with emission peaks of 435 nm, 495 nm, 525 nm and 595 nm through column chromatography separation. The prepared CQDs could be used to fabricated flexible full-color emissive films by mixing with the polymer matrix. Although great progress had achieved in the design of long wavelength emission of carbon dots, mostly by solvothermal method, column chromatography separation and so on, the raw materials used in the synthesis process were mostly toxic organic reagents, and the synthesis steps were complicated and expensive [24]. In this study, multi-color CDs with low toxicity were successfully synthesized by a simple secondary hydrothermal method. The synthesis steps were simple, green and environmentally friendly, there were no complicated separation steps, and no toxic reagents need to be added during the secondary hydrothermal process. The synthesized CDs had excellent optical properties and had good application prospects in multi-color cell imaging and information encryption.

In order to improve information security, optical anti-counterfeiting ink is widely used in banknotes and important documents, but traditional fluorescent materials have high cost and poor stability [25]. Therefore, it is imperative to find a fluorescent material with simple preparation method, low cost and good stability. The properties of CDs have been proved about its good photostability, low toxicity and low cost [26,27]. More importantly, CDs have their unique optical properties. For example, long-life room temperature phosphorescence [28,29], reversible color change triggered by water [30], etc. These unique properties make it difficult to be cracked when applied to anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Therefore, the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots with unique optical properties is of great significance to improve information security.

In this work, a kind of multi-color emission carbon dots with lysosomal targeting was reported for the first time. Using a simple secondary hydrothermal method, we successfully red-shifted the emission wavelength from 450 nm to 560 nm. As the concentration decreased, the emission wavelength was blue shifted, so the synthesized CDs was emitted in the yellow and green regions. CDs emitted different colors under different excitation, providing the possibility for their applications in multi-color cell imaging and information encryption. In addition, the synthesized CDs exhibited obvious quenching of fluorescence intensity at pH 6–8, which had been successfully used to detect pH in tap water.

Dopamine hydrochloride, glutathione, and trimethylol aminomethane were purchased from Aladdin Group Co, Ltd. Citrate amine, ethylenediamine, folic acid,
HCl
,
NaOH
,
NaCl
,
NaS
,
KCl
,
CaCl 2
,
AlCl 3
,
BaCl 2
,
HgCl 2
,
BiCl 3
,
CuCl 2
⋅ 2
H 2
O
,
FeCl 3
⋅ 6
H 2
O
,
MnSO 4
⋅ 2
H 2
O
,
ZnSO 4
⋅ 7
H 2
O
,
MgSO 4
,
Pb
(
NO 3
)
2
,
AgNO 3
,
Zn
(
NO 3
)
2
and other chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagents, Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded in JEOL JEM-2100, operated at 200 kV. UV–vis absorption spectrum and Fluorescence spectrum of CDs was scanned with ultraviolet spectrophotometer (agilent technology, Cary100) and PerkinElmer (LS-55). Absolute quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were measured by the FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The constituent elements and functional groups were identified by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Thermo Nicolet FTS NEXUS). Cell fluorescence images was taken under confocal laser microscopy (FV1200MOE, OLYMPUS, Japan).

The CDs were prepared by secondary hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.40 g ammonium citrate, 0.010 g folic acid and 250 μL ethylenediamine were mixed in 30 mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixed solution was reacted at 200°C for 10 h in an autoclave. The obtained solution was centrifuged in a high-speed centrifuge (12000 rpm, 20 min) and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Next, take 10 mL of the obtained solution and disperse it in 20 mL H2O, and the CDs solution was obtained after the reaction continued for 5 h in an autoclave at 200°C. Eventually, it was purified by further centrifuged and filtered.

The response of CDs to pH was studied using Tris-HCl buffer solution. CDs solution and Tris-HCl buffer solution were mixed in a ratio of 1:9, and then the change of fluorescence intensity was recorded on a spectrometer.

The actual sample was selected from laboratory tap water. It was surprising that the prepared CDs could directly detect pH of tap water. In this process, 900 μL tap water without any treatment was added into 100 μL CDs solution directly. The change of fluorescence intensity in tap water under different pH conditions was recorded by FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer, and then the pH value was calculated by linear equation.

HepG2 cells were cultivated in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well) for 24 h. After that, the medium was replaced with CDs solution of different concentrations (0.125, 0.250, 0.500, 1.00 and 2.00 mg/mL), and re-incubated for 24 h. Then, aspirated the CDs solution, 100 μL CCK-8 solution (the ratio of CCK-8 to medium was 1:10) was added into each well and incubated for 1 h. Eventually, the absorbance was recorded with microplate reader.

The HepG2 cells were cultivated in glass-bottom culture dishes for 24 h to make them adherent. Then, CDs solution (1.00 mg/mL) was added to the dishes and re-incubated for 24 h. Next, the commercial lysosome-stained probes were added for further incubation for 2 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4) to remove the free CDs that did not enter the cells and the fluorescence images were captured using confocal laser microscopy.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology of CDs. The CDs shown in Fig. 1 a were spherical and uniformly distributed. The average particle size of CDs was 2.28 nm and the diameter of CDs distributed in the range of 1.3–3.4 nm after analyzing 100 random particles.

The functional groups compositions of CDs were studied by infrared spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peaks near 3051 cm−1 confirmed the existence of O–H/N–H and C–H groups [31–33]. The peaks at 1643 cm−1 could be ascribed to CO stretching vibration [34,35]. Moreover, C–O stretching vibration could be viewed at 1291 cm−1 and it was obvious that C–N at 1040 cm−1 [33,34]. To elucidate elemental composition of CDs, XPS spectroscopy was further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1c, three peaks could be viewed at 284.8 eV, 400.1 eV and 531.6 eV, which demonstrated CDs were comprised of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [36,37]. The high resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1d) revealed the existence of CC (284.8 ev), C–N/C–O (286.3 ev) and CO (288.2 ev) [21,38]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) proved the existence of pyrrolic N (401.6 ev), pyridinic N (400.1 ev) and N–H (406.7 ev) [33,39]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the groups of CO and C–O were discovered at 531.0 ev and 532.3 ev, respectively [40,41]. The results were consistent with the infrared spectroscopy.

The optical properties of CDs were tested by fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Fig. 2 a shows the emission spectra of CDs with different excitation. It could be observed from the figure that CDs had two emission centers. The maximum emission wavelength gradually red-shifted from 500 nm to 550 nm with the excitation wavelength increased. Fig. 2b depicted the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and the best excitation and maximum emission spectra of CDs. In UV absorption spectrum (green line), a strong peak at 219 nm could be corresponded to π-π* electron transition of CC [42]. In addition, two slight absorption peaks at 358 nm and 450 nm were possibly derived from n-π* electron transition of CO [43,44]. The maximum emission peak was located at 550 nm under optimal excitation at 511 nm. The inset of Fig. 2b shows the images of CDs under 365 nm UV light. It could be observed that CDs solution displayed yellow emission. Surprisingly, when the carbon dots were diluted with deionized water, their fluorescence color also changes, turning blue-green (Fig. 2c).

As shown in Fig. 2d and e, the addition of different metal ions and amino acids had no significant effect on the fluorescence stability of CDs. Meanwhile, Fig. 2f shows the effect of different sodium chloride concentrations on CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs hardly changed even if the concentration of sodium chloride reached 1 mol/L. The results proved the excellent optical stability of CDs in high ionic strength, indicating that it had a good application prospect in physiological environment. Simultaneously, the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of CDs were tested on fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute quantum yield was up to 19.48%, and the average fluorescence lifetime was 7.74 ns, which further demonstrated the good optical properties of CDs.

Abnormal pH values in human body may cause the occurrence of multiple diseases. Therefore, timely monitoring of pH values is of great significance to human health. As shown in Fig. 3 a, Tris-HCl buffer solution with different pH was added to the CDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of CDs almost remained unchanged at pH 3–6. However, it was quenched when the pH was increased from 6 to 8. In addition, Fig. 3b indicated the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of CDs had a good linear relationship with pH (F0 represents the original fluorescence intensity of CDs solution, and F represents the fluorescence intensity in different pH conditions). Its linear equation was F/F0 = −0.1414 pH + 1.8029, and the relative correction coefficient R2 value was 0.9989, it indicating the potential application of CDs in pH monitoring.

The tap water was selected as the actual sample to verify the practicability of CDs to detect pH value. First, the actual pH value of tap water was measured by pH meter, and then measured it again using the method proposed in this work. Without any treatment, the water sample can be directly added into CDs solution for detection. As shown in Table 1 , the measurement results were close to the actual results of the pH meter, indicating that CDs could be applied for the measurement of pH in tap water.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed by CCK-8 method using HepG2 cells. Fig. 4 a showed the viability of HepG2 cells when the concentration of CDs was 0.125, 0.250, 0.500, 1.00 and 2.00 mg/mL. Even if the concentration reached 2.00 mg/mL, the cell viability remained above 70%. The results confirmed that the CDs had low toxicity and was suitable for further imaging. Therefore, CDs were incubated with HepG2 cells to further determine their imaging capabilities. As shown in Fig. 4b, fluorescent images of HepG2 cells (1.00 mg/mL) were taken by confocal microscope under 458 nm and 515 nm laser. HepG2 cells stained with CDs showed green fluorescence and yellow fluorescence respectively under 458 nm and 515 nm lasers. It showed potential application prospects of CDs in multi-color biological imaging.

The targeting ability of CDs was verified by lysosomal co-location assay. Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b shows the confocal images of HepG2 cells stained with commercial lysosomal dyes and CDs, respectively. Fig. 5c shows the merged image and it could be viewed that CDs overlaps with the fluorescent image of lysosomal staining. The Pearson correlation coefficient was 0.6371, demonstrating that the synthesized CDs could target the lysosomes. This statement was further supported by the emission intensity distribution in Fig. 5d.

In the process of research, the concentration-dependent properties of CDs were found. When the CDs solution was diluted with water, the fluorescence color changed from yellow to blue-green under 365 nm UV light. Surprisingly, under the irradiation of 254 nm UV light, the CDs remained yellow fluorescence. According to this characteristic, CDs could be used as fluorescent ink for information encryption and anti counterfeiting. Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b exhibit some numbers written in two kinds of CDs solution. One was the CDs synthesized by secondary hydrothermal method in this work, and another was the ordinary CDs synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method mentioned in section 2.2 of this paper. Under 365 nm UV light, the numbers written with two fluorescence inks exhibited the same fluorescent color (Fig. 6a). However, the fluorescence color of CDs was changed under 254 nm UV light (Fig. 6b). According to this method, the CDs could be used to hide and quickly identify the required information in a simple way, indicating its good application potential in anti-counterfeiting and information security.

In summary, CDs with double emission were fabricated by a simple secondary hydrothermal method. By using this method, the emission wavelength of CDs red-shifted about 100 nm, realizing the conversion from a single short wavelength blue light to polychromatic long-wavelength emission. The Synthetic CDs could be used for multi-color cell imaging and targeting in lysosomes. Moreover, the color-changing effect of CDs under different excitation makes it possible to hide and quickly identify the desired information. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs changed in different pH conditions, and it was successfully used to directly detect the pH in tap water without any treatment. The as-prepared CDs showed good application potential not only in cell imaging and information encryption, but also in pH monitoring.

Xiaoxiao Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Yan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Zheng Fu: Resources, Data curation. Fengling Cui: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan and the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province (U1704170, 192102310300), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education (20A610005, 21A180015) and the doctor initiated project funding of Henan Normal University (QD18083).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing
ACS Sens.

Chem. 60 (2021) 15485-15496, DOI: 10.1021/acs.inorgchem.1c02109.
